questions.question: Explain Scenario-Based Modeling and its advantages in software engineering.
keyPoints: Scenario-based modeling represents system behavior through real-world scenarios or narratives
Provides tangible, relatable descriptions that bridge gap between technical and non-technical stakeholders
Advantages: easy to understand, user-focused, helps identify requirements, facilitates communication, basis for testing
Improves stakeholder engagement by showing concrete examples of system usage

questions.question: Differentiate between <<include>> and <<extend>> relationships in Use Case diagrams with examples.
keyPoints: Include: Used to extract common behavior into a separate use case to avoid duplication - mandatory relationship
Extend: Used to add optional or exceptional behavior to a base use case - conditional relationship
Example Include: 'Process Payment' included in both 'Place Order' and 'Renew Subscription'
Example Extend: 'Print Receipt' extends 'Withdraw Cash' only when customer requests receipt
Direction: Include arrow from base to included; Extend arrow from extending to base

questions.question: Describe the components of a complete Use Case description.
keyPoints: Use Case Name: Descriptive name of the functionality
Actors: Primary and secondary actors involved
Preconditions: Conditions that must be true before use case starts
Basic Flow: Primary path describing normal successful scenario (numbered steps)
Alternative Flows: Secondary paths for variations and exceptions
Postconditions: Conditions that must be true after successful completion
Business Rules: Any applicable rules and constraints
Extensions: Alternative scenarios that extend or modify basic flow

questions.question: Explain the difference between Primary Actor and Secondary Actor in Use Case diagrams.
keyPoints: Primary Actor: Initiates the use case to achieve a specific goal; represents the main user of the system
Secondary Actor: Participates in the use case but does not initiate it; provides assistance to the system
Primary actors have a goal that the system helps achieve; secondary actors are systems or services
Example in ATM: Customer is primary actor for 'Withdraw Cash'; Bank system is secondary actor that verifies account
Identification: Ask 'Who starts this use case?' - that's the primary actor

questions.question: What is a System Boundary in Use Case diagrams? How is it useful in requirements gathering?
keyPoints: System boundary is a rectangle that defines the scope of the system in a Use Case diagram
Everything inside the boundary represents functionalities provided by the system
Everything outside represents external entities (actors) that interact with the system
Helps define project scope and avoid scope creep
Facilitates clear communication about what is and isn't part of the system
Allows stakeholders to clearly understand system boundaries and responsibilities

questions.question: Explain the process of identifying Use Cases from system requirements.
keyPoints: Start by identifying all potential actors of the system
For each actor, identify their goals and what they want to achieve
Look for verbs indicating system actions (process, create, manage, calculate)
Identify system responses to user inputs and requests
Find business processes that deliver value to actors
Analyze functional requirements for recurring patterns to extract common behaviors
Validate use cases by ensuring each provides a complete unit of value to an actor
Refine use cases through iteration and stakeholder feedback

questions.question: Draw and explain a complete Use Case diagram for an Online Shopping System. Include at least 4 use cases, 2 actors, and demonstrate include, extend, and generalization relationships.
keyPoints: Use Case Diagram with: Actors (Customer, Administrator), Use Cases (Browse Products, Add to Cart, Place Order, Process Payment, Manage Products, View Reports)
Include: 'Process Payment' included in 'Place Order' - payment is mandatory for order
Extend: 'Add to Cart' can extend to 'Apply Coupon' when customer has coupon; 'View Reports' extends for different report types
Generalization: Customer can generalize to Registered Customer and Guest Customer; Admin can generalize to Super Admin
System boundary rectangle encloses all use cases
Explain each relationship and its purpose
Discuss how the diagram captures functional requirements and system scope

questions.question: Explain the scenario-based modeling approach in detail. How does it help in bridging the communication gap between technical and non-technical stakeholders? Provide a practical example.
keyPoints: Scenario-based modeling uses real-world narratives/scenarios to describe system behavior
Unlike abstract specifications, it provides concrete examples stakeholders can relate to
Steps: Identify actors → Identify use cases → Describe scenarios → Detail use case descriptions
Advantages: Easy to understand (no technical jargon), user-centric, facilitates communication
Bridges gap: Technical teams understand what's needed; non-technical stakeholders understand what will be delivered
Example: Instead of 'System shall process transactions', describe 'Customer inserts card, enters PIN, selects amount, receives cash and receipt'
Scenarios act as shared language between developers, testers, and business users
Serves as basis for acceptance testing and user manuals

questions.question: Write a detailed Use Case description for the 'Withdraw Cash' use case in an ATM system. Include all components like preconditions, postconditions, basic flow, and at least 2 alternative flows.
keyPoints: Use Case Name: Withdraw Cash
Primary Actor: Customer
Secondary Actor: Bank System (for verification)
Preconditions: Customer has valid bank card, card is not blocked, account has sufficient balance

Basic Flow: 1. Customer inserts card → 2. System prompts for PIN → 3. Customer enters PIN → 4. System verifies PIN → 5. Main menu displayed → 6. Customer selects 'Withdraw' → 7. Customer enters amount → 8. System verifies balance → 9. System dispenses cash → 10. Customer takes cash → 11. System dispenses receipt → 12. Transaction complete
Alternative Flow 1 (Invalid PIN): After step 3, if PIN is wrong, display error, allow retry (max 3 attempts), block card if exceeded
Alternative Flow 2 (Insufficient Balance): After step 7, if amount exceeds balance, display message, return to main menu
Alternative Flow 3 (No Receipt): After step 10, customer may skip receipt
Postconditions: Cash dispensed (or appropriate message), account debited, transaction logged

questions.question: Explain all types of relationships in Use Case diagrams with suitable examples. Discuss when to use each relationship.
keyPoints: Association: Connects actor to use case (solid line); shows communication between actor and use case; can be bidirectional
Include: Dashed arrow with <<include>> from base to included; mandatory, always used; example: 'Calculate Total' included in 'Process Order' for all orders
Extend: Dashed arrow with <<extend>> from extending to base; optional/conditional; example: 'Print Receipt' extends 'Withdraw Cash' only when requested

Generalization: Solid arrow with triangle head from child to parent; inheritance; example: 'Registered User' inherits from 'User'; use case generalization like 'View Report' generalizes 'View Sales Report' and 'View Inventory Report'
When to use: Use Association for all actor-use case interactions; Use Include for reusable common behaviors; Use Extend for optional/exceptional behaviors; Use Generalization for related variations
